content.text: Reasoning capability refers to how deeply a model can analyse a problem before producing an answer. All Claude models reason implicitly during token generation — this is standard behaviour with no additional configuration. For harder problems,

gives the model an explicit internal reasoning budget: a token allowance it may spend on scratch-work before producing any visible output. Understanding when reasoning depth matters, and how to control it, determines whether an agent makes correct decisions on complex tasks.

attrs.alt: Reasoning capability — standard output vs extended thinking

content.text: In a standard model call the model reasons implicitly: it attends to the full context and generates tokens in one pass. For the majority of agent tasks — tool dispatch, data extraction, code edits, result summarisation — this is sufficient and is the right default:
— the model's chain of thought is not exposed in the response. What you see in
is the model's final output, not its deliberation.
— reading a file, calling a search tool, appending a result, running a test — none of these require extended thinking. Standard mode is faster and cheaper.
Extended thinking is enabled via the
parameter. The model receives an explicit token budget for internal reasoning; those tokens appear in the response as
Max tokens the model may spend on reasoning
Must be greater than budget_tokens + expected output

Thinking blocks are in the response for transparency — you can inspect the model's reasoning for debugging. They do not need to be shown to end users and should not be re-injected into subsequent user turns.

Extended thinking is most valuable on planning and architecture steps, not on mechanical execution steps. A loop that applies thinking selectively — hard steps get a budget, simple steps run standard — achieves better cost-quality balance than applying it uniformly:
— keyword signals are a fast heuristic; replace with a meta-model call for higher-precision routing in production.

— when you append the assistant turn to the messages list for a conversation that used extended thinking, the thinking blocks must be present. The API requires them for coherent multi-turn extended thinking.
— use this when you want the model's answer without the reasoning trace.

# Thinking budget selection guide
# budget_tokens controls how many tokens the model may spend on internal reasoning.
# Higher budgets improve quality on hard tasks at proportionally higher cost.
#
# Practical thresholds (Sonnet 4.6):
#   1 024  — light: simple classification with verification, short code review
#   4 096  — medium: multi-file debugging, API design, test coverage analysis
#   8 192  — high: complex system design, security analysis, algorithm optimisation
#  16 000  — very high: novel architecture, hard mathematical problems
#  64 000  — max (Sonnet); 32 000 max (Opus): exhaustive reasoning tasks
#
# Rule: start low and increase until quality is acceptable.
# Doubling the budget does not double quality — gains diminish past ~8k for most tasks.
#
# max_tokens must exceed budget_tokens:
#   max_tokens = budget_tokens + expected_output_tokens + safety_margin
#   A safe formula: max_tokens = budget_tokens * 2
#
# Example for a hard task with ~1000 token output:
#   budget_tokens = 8000
#   max_tokens = 16000
— this is a reasonable default for medium-difficulty problems. Increase only if quality is insufficient.
— for most tasks, budgets beyond 8k produce diminishing quality improvements. Very hard mathematical or logical problems benefit from higher budgets.
— the model needs room for the thinking block
the output. A safe formula is
Thinking Blocks and the Messages List

Extended thinking creates an important constraint: when a conversation includes a turn with thinking blocks, subsequent API calls in that conversation must include those blocks in the messages list. Stripping them breaks the conversation invariant. However, thinking blocks should never be injected into fresh system prompts or new user turns:

import anthropic


def strip_thinking_blocks(content: list) -> list:
    """
    Remove thinking blocks from an assistant turn before storing or logging.
    Thinking blocks are large and not needed for continuing the loop —
    the model already incorporated them into its text/tool_use output.

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT strip thinking blocks from turns you pass back to the
    API in the messages list. The API requires thinking blocks in prior turns
    when extended thinking is enabled.
    """
    return [
        block for block in content
        if not (hasattr(block, "type") and block.type == "thinking")
    ]


def should_use_thinking(task: str, step: int) -> bool:
    """
    Use extended thinking selectively — only on hard steps, not every call.
    Saves cost on straightforward tool dispatch and result collection steps.
    """
    hard_keywords = ["design", "analyse", "plan", "trade-off", "decide"]
    is_hard = any(kw in task.lower() for kw in hard_keywords)
    is_early_step = step <= 2  # planning steps benefit most
    return is_hard or is_early_step
Extended thinking and tool use are fully compatible. The model can use its thinking budget to plan which tools to call and in what order before issuing any
blocks. A common pattern applies thinking only to the first (planning) step and reverts to standard mode for subsequent execution steps:
Thinking blocks carry across tool-use steps
— when the loop appends the assistant turn containing thinking blocks, the API sees the full reasoning chain on the next call.
Do not re-enable thinking for every step
— the planning step is where the budget pays off. Tool dispatch and result collection steps do not benefit.

Thinking tokens are billed at the same rate as output tokens. A standard call with a 500-token answer costs very differently from an extended thinking call where the model uses 6000 tokens of its reasoning budget:

# Token cost comparison: standard vs extended thinking
# (approximate, for reference — actual pricing varies by model)
#
# Standard call (Sonnet 4.6):
#   Input:  1000 tokens (context)
#   Output:  500 tokens (answer)
#   Total:  1500 tokens billed
#
# Extended thinking call (Sonnet 4.6, budget_tokens=8000):
#   Input:    1000 tokens (context)
#   Thinking: 6000 tokens (model uses 6000 of its 8000 budget)
#   Output:    500 tokens (answer)
#   Total:    7500 tokens billed  (~5× more than standard)
#
# Rule of thumb: use extended thinking when the quality gain on a hard
# task justifies the 3–10× cost increase. For most tasks it does not.
#
# Measuring actual thinking usage:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=10000,
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 8000},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Design a rate limiting strategy."}],
)

thinking_chars = sum(
    len(b.thinking) for b in response.content if b.type == "thinking"
)
output_tokens = response.usage.output_tokens
print(f"Thinking chars: {thinking_chars}")
print(f"Total output tokens: {output_tokens}")
print(f"Input tokens: {response.usage.input_tokens}")
— extended thinking on a task that uses half its 8000-token budget adds roughly 4× the output token cost.
— the model does not always use its full budget. Log
(or the output token count) to understand where spending occurs.
— applying extended thinking to one hard planning step out of twenty total steps adds ~5% to the total run cost while improving quality where it matters most.

System prompt instructions can guide reasoning quality even without extended thinking. Asking the model to reason step-by-step, consider alternatives, or express confidence produces more reliable outputs on complex problems:

# Prompt patterns that improve reasoning quality
# These work with both standard and extended thinking.

# 1. Ask for step-by-step reasoning explicitly
STEP_BY_STEP = """
Before answering, reason through this problem step by step.
Show your reasoning, then give a final answer.
"""

# 2. Ask the model to consider multiple approaches
CONSIDER_ALTERNATIVES = """
Consider at least two different approaches to this problem,
identify trade-offs, then recommend one with justification.
"""

# 3. Ask for confidence and caveats
WITH_CONFIDENCE = """
Provide your best answer. State your confidence level (high/medium/low)
and list any assumptions or areas of uncertainty.
"""

# 4. Structure complex tasks with explicit phases
PHASED = """
Work in two phases:
Phase 1 — Analyse: identify the key constraints and requirements.
Phase 2 — Design: propose a solution that satisfies them.
Label each phase clearly.
"""

# 5. Combine with extended thinking for hardest problems
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=16000,
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 10000},
    system=CONSIDER_ALTERNATIVES,
    messages=[{
        "role": "user",
        "content": "How should we shard this Postgres database as we scale to 100M rows?",
    }],
)

These prompt patterns complement extended thinking — they improve the structure of the reasoning, while the thinking budget improves its depth. Using both together yields the best results on hard problems.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# WRONG — stripping thinking blocks before passing messages back to the API
def bad_loop(task: str) -> str:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=12000,
        thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 6000},
        messages=messages,
    )
    # WRONG: stripping thinking blocks here breaks the next API call
    text_only = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "text"]
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": text_only})
    # The next call to the API will error — thinking blocks are missing
    return ""


# RIGHT — preserve the full response.content in the messages list
def good_loop(task: str) -> str:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=12000,
        thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 6000},
        messages=messages,
    )
    # Preserve all blocks including thinking
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
    # Now the next API call will succeed
    return next((b.text for b in response.content if b.type == "text"), "")
Never strip thinking blocks from messages you pass back
— the API requires them in prior turns for coherent multi-turn extended thinking. Only strip for display or storage purposes after the conversation ends.
Do not use extended thinking for every step
— applying a large thinking budget to a file-read or a simple classification step wastes tokens with no quality gain.

Reasoning capability in Claude models operates at two levels: implicit reasoning during standard token generation, and explicit extended thinking via a configurable token budget. Standard reasoning is the right default for most agent steps — it is fast, cheap, and sufficient for tool dispatch, extraction, and summarisation. Extended thinking is worth enabling for hard steps: system design, security analysis, algorithm optimisation, and other tasks where deliberation improves quality. Configure

above the budget, and preserve thinking blocks in the messages list when continuing a multi-turn conversation that used extended thinking. Apply thinking selectively — not to every step — to keep cost and latency under control.
